title: She Conducted Herself Blamelessly
subtitle: Syra of Oxyrhynchus, the Nile Valley, ca. 20-50 CE

sections.body_html:
ἀνέγκλητον ἐμαυτὴν ἐν ἁπᾶσι παρειχόμην — “I conducted myself blamelessly in all respects.”

She wrote it about herself. Or rather, someone held the pen while she spoke. The phrase was conventional — it appears in marriage contracts across centuries of Egyptian papyri, a formula as standardized as “to have and to hold.” But Syra, daughter of Theon, was not reciting a contract. She was filing a complaint.1 And in the space between the formula and its deployment — between what the words were designed to say and what she needed them to do — lies the story of a marriage that consumed everything she had and left her with nothing but the legal vocabulary to describe her ruin.

sections.title: I. The petition
sections.body_html:
The document is a single sheet of papyrus, now catalogued as P.Oxy. 2.281, found in the rubbish dumps of ancient Oxyrhynchus — modern el-Bahnasa, in Middle Egypt, about 160 kilometers south of Cairo.2 It was excavated by Bernard Grenfell and Arthur Hunt in 1897, part of the vast haul of texts that would eventually yield over five thousand published documents from this one provincial city.

Let her speak. The full text, translated from the Greek:

To Heraclides, priest, chief justice, superintendent of the chrematistae and the other courts, from Syra, daughter of Theon. I married Sarapion, bringing him by cession a dowry amounting to 200 drachmae of silver. As he was destitute of means I received him into my parents’ house, and I for my part conducted myself blamelessly in all respects. But Sarapion, having squandered my dowry as he pleased, continually ill-treated and insulted me, using violence towards me, and depriving me of the necessaries of life; finally he deserted me leaving me in a state of destitution. I therefore beg you to order him to be brought before you, in order that he may be compelled perforce to pay back my dowry increased by half its amount. This petition is without prejudice to any other claims which I have or may have against him.3

That is the whole document. No date survives, but the editors placed it between 20 and 50 CE — the early decades of Roman imperial rule in Egypt, when Tiberius or Claudius sat on the throne and the Nile valley was adjusting to a new kind of governance layered atop very old ways of managing property, marriage, and complaint.4

We have the petition. We do not have the outcome.

sections.title: II. The woman
sections.body_html:
Her name was Syra — Syra — which means “the Syrian,” or “woman from Syria.”5 The name does not necessarily mean she or her family had recently arrived from Syria. By the first century CE, ethnic names had become conventional across Egyptian communities, passed down through generations long after any real connection to the place of origin had faded. A woman named Syra in Oxyrhynchus might have had a great-grandmother from Antioch, or she might simply have had a great-grandmother from Oxyrhynchus who liked the name. We do not know.

Her father was Theon. The patronymic — Syras tēs Theōnos, “Syra daughter of Theon” — was her legal identity, the name by which the courts would know her.6 Theon was among the most common names in Oxyrhynchus: a marriage contract from the early second century records a husband called Theon; another document names a chief justice called Theon; land cessions involve a man called Theon son of Demetrius.7 The name tells us little about Syra’s specific family.

What tells us more is what she owned.

She had a house. “My parents’ house,” she calls it — ta tōn goneōn mou oikētēria — using the plural goneōn, “parents,” which might mean both her father and mother were alive, or might simply be a conventional way of describing the family home.8 She had 200 silver drachmae for a dowry. And she had, at least initially, the expectation that marriage would be a partnership conducted on roughly equal terms.

We do not know her age, her occupation, whether she could read, whether she had siblings, whether she was beautiful or plain, strong or fragile. The papyrus does not care about these things. It cares about money, conduct, and remedy.

sections.title: III. The weight of 200 drachmae
sections.body_html:
To understand what Syra staked on this marriage, you need to feel the sum.

A weaver’s loom — the basic tool of the most common trade in Oxyrhynchus — cost 20 drachmae.9 A linen tunic, the kind a weaver might wear to work, was worth 8.10 A month’s extortion from a corrupt tax collector — the kind of sum that would drive a working man to petition the authorities — ran to about 4 drachmae per month, or 24 over half a year.11 When a weaver named Tryphon married his second wife Saraeus around 37 CE, her entire dowry — cash, gold earrings, a milk-white robe — amounted to 72 drachmae.12 An amicable divorce settlement in 96 CE returned 400 drachmae to a woman named Thaesis.13 A wealthy family in 127 CE provided a dowry of 4,100 drachmae — twenty times Syra’s.14 A slave girl aged eight was valued at 640 drachmae — more than three times what Syra brought to her marriage.15

Syra’s 200 drachmae, then, was neither rich nor poor. It was the dowry of a respectable artisan family, the kind that owned a house but not land, that could afford to give a daughter a meaningful sum but not an extravagant one. Ten looms. Twenty-five tunics. Nearly three times what a weaver’s wife brought to her marriage in the same city in the same decade. For a family that also opened their home to a destitute son-in-law, 200 drachmae was a serious investment.

The dowry was given “by cession” — kata synchōrēsin — meaning it was registered through an official notarial process, probably at the local notary’s office or via the archidikastes’s contract registration system.16 This was not a handshake arrangement. The money was documented, witnessed, filed. Syra and her family knew that dowries could be contested, squandered, denied. They took precautions.

The precautions failed.

sections.title: IV. The husband
sections.body_html:
His name was Sarapion. Just Sarapion — no patronymic, no father’s name, no identifying detail beyond the name itself.17 In a city where legal documents routinely identified people by their father’s name, their quarter, their trade, their age, and sometimes even their scars, this absence is striking. Either the document is damaged where his patronymic would have appeared, or Syra did not think — or did not care — to specify which Sarapion she meant. There were many. The name, derived from the god Sarapis, was one of the most common in Oxyrhynchus. A single marriage contract from 127 CE names a groom descended from four generations of Sarapions.18

What we know about him comes entirely from Syra’s complaint, and it is damning.

He was liton pantelōs onta — “utterly destitute.”19 The word litos means stripped, bare, impoverished. Not merely poor but without resources of any kind. He had no house, no workshop, no capital. Syra received him — epidexamenē auton — into her parents’ home. The verb carries a note of hospitality, even generosity: she took him in. The normal pattern in Greco-Egyptian marriages was for the wife to move into the husband’s household. Syra’s marriage inverted this completely. She brought the money. She brought the house. He brought himself.

There is a tantalizing possibility that connects Sarapion’s name to a wider family network. A contemporary document, P.Oxy. 2.285, records a petition from “Sarapion, son of Theon” — a weaver living in the Gymnasium Square quarter of Oxyrhynchus — who was being extorted by a corrupt ex-tax collector named Apollophanes.20 Syra’s petition identifies her as “Syra, daughter of Theon.” The patronymic match, the same city, the same decades — if her father Theon is the same Theon who fathered the weaver Sarapion, then Syra had a brother in the weaving trade, and the Sarapion she married was a different man entirely. This is not impossible — the name was extremely common — but the coincidence of patronymic, city, period, and social class is suggestive. Perhaps the brother could have told us more about the family’s circumstances. But the connection cannot be confirmed. Theon was too common a name for patronymics alone to establish kinship.21

sections.title: V. The escalation
sections.body_html:
Syra’s account of the marriage is told in a single devastating sentence in the Greek — a sentence that moves from virtue to violence with the structural precision of a legal argument and the emotional force of someone who has rehearsed the telling:

ho de Sarapiōn katachrēsamenos tēi phernēi eis hon ebouleto logon ou dieleipen kakouchōn me kai hybrizōn kai tas cheiras epipherōn kai tōn anankaiōn endeē kathistás, hysteron de enkatélipe me lithēn kathestōsan.22

“But Sarapion, having squandered my dowry as he pleased, continually ill-treated and insulted me, using violence towards me, and depriving me of the necessaries of life; finally he deserted me leaving me in a state of destitution.”

Each term carries legal weight. This was not venting. This was a case.

Katachrēsamenos tēi phernēi — “having squandered the dowry.” The prefix kata- intensifies the verb: he did not merely use the dowry, he used it up, consumed it, misused it willfully.23 Marriage contracts routinely prohibited husbands from disposing of dowry property without the wife’s consent. A contract from 127 CE explicitly stipulated that the husband must not “sell or mortgage or otherwise dispose of his property” without the bride’s agreement.24 Sarapion had breached this fundamental obligation.

Kakouchōn — “ill-treating.” This was not generic language. The word appears in marriage contracts as a specifically prohibited behavior. A contract from 81-96 CE includes the clause mēde kakouchein autēn — “nor to ill-treat her.”25 The same root, the same legal register. When Syra used this word, she was invoking a contractual standard, not merely describing her suffering.

Hybrizōn — “insulting.” The term hybris carried far more weight in Greek than its English translation suggests. It denoted an assault on another person’s dignity and honor, a violation of their standing as a human being. In Athenian law, hybris had been a prosecutable offense regardless of the victim’s status.26 In Syra’s petition, the word signals that Sarapion did not merely hurt her. He degraded her.

Tas cheiras epipherōn — literally “laying on hands,” the standard Greek legal formula for physical violence.27 The phrase is clinical, almost bureaucratic. It does not describe what he did with those hands. It does not need to. The formula was understood.

Tōn anankaiōn endeē kathistás — “making me lacking in the necessities.”28 Food, clothing, shelter — the anankaia that marriage contracts required husbands to provide “in proportion to their means.”29 Even after squandering her dowry, Sarapion was obligated to provide for Syra. He did not.

And then the final term: enkatélipe me — “he abandoned me.”30 Not a negotiated separation. Not a divorce by mutual consent. He left. And the word Syra uses to describe her condition after his departure is the same word she used to describe his condition when he arrived: lithēn — destitute, stripped bare.31 He came to her with nothing. He left her with nothing. The symmetry is brutal, and perhaps intentional.

sections.title: VI. The weavers' world
sections.body_html:
To understand the world Syra and Sarapion inhabited, we can look at the family that left the richest paper trail from first-century Oxyrhynchus: the household of Tryphon, son of Dionysius, a weaver.

Tryphon’s archive spans decades and is one of the best-documented artisan records from Roman Egypt.32 He married his first wife, Demetrous, daughter of Heraclides, sometime before 29 CE, and the marriage ended badly — she left, taking his property with her. He petitioned the local strategus for its return, using language that mirrors Syra’s petition in reverse: where she was the abandoned wife seeking her dowry, he was the abandoned husband seeking his stolen goods.33 Both petitions close with the identical formula: “This petition is without prejudice to the other claims which I have or may have against…”34 The legal machinery of Oxyrhynchus had a template for broken marriages, and both Syra and Tryphon knew it. Or their scribes did.

By 37 CE, Tryphon had remarried. His second wife, Saraeus, daughter of Apion, brought a dowry of 72 drachmae — 40 in cash, 20 in gold earrings, 12 for a milk-white robe — registered “through the bank of Sarapion, son of Kleandrus” at the Serapeum.35 The marriage document contains a clause that bears directly on Syra’s case: if Tryphon failed to repay the dowry, he would forfeit the sum “with the addition of half its amount” — meth’ hēmiolias — the same fifty-percent penalty that Syra claims in her petition.36

Tryphon’s life continued to generate paperwork. In 44 CE, his mother Thamounion reported his son from the first marriage — also named Thoönis — as having “removed abroad.”37 The son “has no trade” and “possesses no means,” Thamounion told the authorities — language remarkably close to Syra’s description of her husband as “utterly destitute.”38 The registration was not sentimental; it was fiscal. If Thoönis had absconded, the family would otherwise be liable for his taxes. People disappeared in Roman Egypt. Someone had to account for their absence.

In 52 CE, Tryphon himself was examined in Alexandria and released from compulsory public service: “suffering from cataract and shortness of sight.”39 In 54 CE, he bought a new loom — three weavers’ cubits less two palms, with two rollers and two beams — for 20 drachmae, transacted through a bank at the Serapeum.40 In 66 CE, he apprenticed his son Thoenis (by Saraeus) to another weaver, Ptolemaeus, for one year, at five drachmae a month for food, twelve drachmae for clothing at completion, with a penalty of one drachma for each missed day and 100 drachmae for early withdrawal.41

This was the world of Oxyrhynchus weavers: tight networks, modest sums, hereditary trades, and the constant threat of economic ruin from tax collectors, military service, and family members who disappeared. Whether Syra was a weaver’s daughter we cannot say. But her 200-drachma dowry placed her in the artisan class — better off than Tryphon’s wife Saraeus, worse off than the families who negotiated dowries in the thousands.

sections.title: VII. The tax collector
sections.body_html:
While Syra’s marriage was dissolving, the weavers of Oxyrhynchus were under siege.

A former tax collector named Apollophanes — ex-collector of the trade tax upon weavers — had been systematically extorting the city’s artisans.42 Sarapion son of Theon, the weaver from Gymnasium Square, reported that Apollophanes had “using great violence seized from me a linen tunic which I was wearing, worth 8 drachmae” and then extorted a further 24 drachmae over six months, at two drachmae per month.43 Another weaver, Alexandrus son of Apollonius, from the quarter of the Square of Thoëris, reported that Apollophanes had extorted 16 drachmae from him “among other people” — suggesting the abuse was widespread and targeting an entire trade.44

Both petitions were addressed to the strategus Tiberius Claudius Pasion, which dates them to the reign of Claudius, roughly 41-54 CE — overlapping with the likely date of Syra’s petition.45 The picture is of a community under economic pressure from multiple directions: corrupt officials extracting money from below, while marriages and family arrangements strained under the weight of too little shared among too many.

What did the “necessaries of life” that Sarapion denied Syra actually look like? A household account from Oxyrhynchus, roughly contemporary, itemizes the texture of daily survival: “for the weaver’s breakfast 1 obol; beer for the weaver 1 obol; leeks for the weaver’s breakfast 1 obol… cost of weaving a cloak 1 drachma 2 obols… pure bread for the children 1/2 obol… milk for the children 1/2 obol.”46 Obols. Fractions of obols. The margins were measured in the smallest coins available. When Syra said she was deprived of anankaia, she meant someone stopped buying the leeks and the bread and the milk.

sections.title: VIII. The court she chose
sections.body_html:
Syra did not petition the local strategus, as the weavers did. She went higher. She addressed her complaint to “Heraclides, priest, chief justice, superintendent of the chrematistae and the other courts” — Hērakleidēi hierei kai archidikastēi kai pros tēi epimeleiāi tōn chrēmatistōn kai tōn allōn kritēriōn.47

The archidikastes was based in Alexandria. He was the highest judicial authority in the province after the Roman prefect himself. The title combined religious and judicial functions: the archidikastes was always a priest, always the superintendent of the chrematistae courts, always possessed of jurisdiction over all of Egypt.48 The title formula remained stable for centuries. In 81 CE, the archidikastes was a man named Pallas, who handled loan documents with hemiolion penalties and provisions for “arrest and detention.”49 After 57 CE, an archidikastes named Theon — the same name as Syra’s father — handled an 800-drachma dowry settlement for Oxyrhynchus families.50 In 267 CE, Aurelius Gaius, also called Harpocration, was still using the identical title formula.51

The chrematistae whom the archidikastes supervised were originally itinerant judges, created by Ptolemy II Philadelphus as one of three court systems in Hellenistic Egypt.52 Greek courts called dikasteria handled disputes under Greek law; Egyptian courts called laokritai judged under Egyptian law; and the chrematistae — literally “those in charge of handling transactions” — managed royal and administrative matters. Over time, the chrematistae absorbed the other two systems and gained blanket jurisdiction.53 By Syra’s day, they were the primary court system, administered from Alexandria by the archidikastes.

Why did Syra go to the top? Perhaps because her dowry had been registered “by cession” through the archidikastes’s office, and she needed to return to the same jurisdiction. Perhaps because the strategus had already been approached and had failed to act. Perhaps because the severity of her case — abuse, violence, desertion, total financial ruin — warranted the highest available authority.54 We do not know. But the choice suggests either legal sophistication or legal desperation. Possibly both.

sections.title: IX. The hemiolion
sections.body_html:
Syra asked for her dowry back “increased by half its amount” — syn hēmioliāi.55 Two hundred drachmae plus the fifty-percent penalty would make 300.

This was not arbitrary. The hemiolion — the half-again penalty — was one of the most deeply embedded provisions in Greco-Egyptian contract law.

Contract after contract contains the clause. Tryphon’s marriage to Saraeus in 37 CE: “I will forfeit to you the said sum with the addition of half its amount.”56 A marriage contract from 127 CE: “the husband shall repay the 4,100 drachmae of the dowry or forfeit this amount increased by one half.”57 A contract naming Theon as the groom: “or shall pay with the addition of half” — ē apoteisatō meth’ hēmiolias.58 A loan from 81 CE, filed through the archidikastes Pallas: the borrower was “liable to arrest and detention until she pays in full… the loan of 38 drachmae of silver increased by one half.”59

The penalty served two purposes. It compensated the wronged party for the delay and inconvenience of pursuing recovery. And it deterred default — if you knew that failing to return the dowry would cost you fifty percent more, you had incentive to pay promptly. In theory.

In Syra’s case, the theory confronted a man who had already squandered the original 200 drachmae. Whether he could pay 300 — whether he could pay anything — is another question. The petition asks that Sarapion “be brought before you” and “compelled perforce” — epanankasthēi synechomenos — language that invokes the coercive power of the court, including the possibility of arrest and detention.60 Syra was not asking politely. She was asking the most powerful judge in Egypt to seize her husband and squeeze money out of him.

sections.title: X. The woman without a guardian
sections.body_html:
There is one more thing about Syra’s petition that scholars have noted. It is perhaps the most remarkable feature of all.

She appears to act without a male guardian.

In Greco-Roman Egypt, women generally required a kyrios — a guardian — to conduct legal business.61 When Thaesis divorced her husband Petosarapis in 96 CE, she acted “with her guardian her step-father Onnophris.”62 When Ammonarion settled her late husband’s dowry obligation after 57 CE, she acted “with their guardian, the half brother of Ammonarion.”63 When Julia Heracla transferred property to her daughter in 95 CE, she acted “with the guardian assigned to her by the (instructions) issued by Gaius Septimius Vegetus, the late praefect.”64 When Sieapis registered property around 76 CE, she acted “with her son Ariston son of Diogenes as guardian.”65

Syra’s petition mentions no guardian at all.

Several explanations are possible. The guardian’s name may have appeared in a lost portion of the papyrus. Syra may have possessed the ius liberorum — the “right of three children” — which under Roman law exempted women who had borne three legitimate children from the requirement of guardianship.66 A later Oxyrhynchus document shows a woman “acting without a guardian by the ius liberorum,” proving the exemption was used.67 Or perhaps the guardianship requirement was simply not enforced for petitions as opposed to contracts and property transfers.

Whatever the explanation, the effect is striking. Syra speaks in her own voice. She does not introduce a male intermediary. She names herself, states her case, and demands her remedy. The Greek is first person throughout: synebiōsa — “I married”; epidexamenē — “I received him”; pareichomēn — “I conducted myself”; axiō — “I beg.”68 Whether or not a scribe held the pen, and whether or not a guardian hovered in the background, the petition presents a woman acting for herself in the highest court in Egypt.

sections.title: XI. The mirror
sections.body_html:
One papyrus shelf away from Syra’s petition, in the same volume of the Oxyrhynchus Papyri, sits the complaint of a man named Tryphon — the same weaver whose archive we have already traced — about his first wife, Demetrous.

The parallel is almost too neat. Where Syra is the abandoned wife petitioning for her dowry, Tryphon is the abandoned husband petitioning for his stolen property. Where Syra says “I for my part conducted myself blamelessly in all respects,” Tryphon says “I for my part provided for my wife in a manner that exceeded my resources.”69 Both claim the moral high ground. Both portray themselves as the party who gave more than they received. Both were probably telling the truth as they understood it.

Tryphon’s complaint is dated to somewhere between 29 and 37 CE, making it roughly contemporary with Syra’s petition.70 The two documents may have passed through the same administrative offices. The two families lived in the same city, in the same decade, in the same economic stratum. They may have known each other. They may have attended the same festivals at the Serapeum, bought bread from the same market, heard each other’s names in the quarter.

We may imagine this. We cannot prove it.

But the contrast between the two petitions illuminates the asymmetry of marriage in Roman Egypt. Tryphon complains that his wife took his property — a list of goods that begins with an item worth 40 drachmae.71 Syra complains that her husband took her dowry, beat her, starved her, and left. Both petitions end with the same formula. The scales of grievance are not remotely comparable.

sections.title: XII. Other women, other petitions
sections.body_html:
Syra was not alone. The petition was a genre, and women used it.

Around 150 BCE, a woman named Senyris had petitioned the chief judge about a man she had been “living with under a contract of maintenance.” “He threw me out,” she wrote, “and he doesn’t even feed or clothe us. And on top of that, he has taken on another woman.”72 Different century, different legal framework, same complaint: a woman discarded, a man who stopped providing.

In 82 CE, a woman named Philumene pressed a debtor for 2,000 drachmae of silver. The petition was filed by the man’s brother-in-law, who said Philumene was “continually pressing me to repay.”73 Women as creditors, women as economic actors, women whose pressure could compel men to petition courts on their behalf.

In 144 CE, a woman named Diemous reported a burglary: “I shut up the door of my house and the door of the terrace, and on my return I found that a box had been unfastened and that there had been abstracted from it two gold bracelets of the weight of four minae, a gold figure of Bes, and two large silver bracelets.”74 She had “some suspicion against my neighbours Heras son of Kalathus, weaver, and those working with him, because my house is easy of access from the house of Heras.” A woman identifying her suspect, naming his trade, describing the architectural vulnerability.

In 45 CE — very close to Syra’s own time — another petition from Oxyrhynchus described a violent confrontation during the seizure of a slave, with “a severe and violent attack upon myself by him and those by whom he was surrounded.”75 Also addressed to the strategus Tiberius Claudius Pasion, the same official who received the weavers’ complaints.

A letter from 22 CE — also contemporary — gives a glimpse of the daily texture. A man named Sarapion writes to his brother Dorion from Alexandria: “I learned from some fishermen that Secunda’s house has been searched and that my house has been searched.” He is being pressured to enter the service of an official. He asks his brother to “look after Demetrous and our father Dorion.”76 The mention of a Demetrous echoes the name of Tryphon’s first wife. The Nile valley was small enough that the same names circulated through generation after generation, each Sarapion and Demetrous and Theon a fresh life lived under a recycled name.

sections.title: XIII. Dionysia's precedent
sections.body_html:
The most extensive evidence that women could win in the courts comes from a later case: the legal battle of Dionysia, daughter of Chaeremon, against her own father, who attempted to dissolve her marriage and reclaim her dowry.77

The case generated a dossier of precedents and judicial opinions that is one of the most important documents for understanding women’s legal rights in Roman Egypt. A prefect named Flavius Titianus, hearing a similar case, ruled: “The decision depends upon the question, with whom the wife wishes to live.”78 A lawyer’s opinion stated: “Since Dionysia has been given away by her father in marriage, she is no longer in his power.”79 Another precedent held that “if the marriage was not cancelled the father had no power over the dowry any more than over the daughter whom he had given in marriage.”80

The law, at least in principle, protected women like Syra. Dowries were legally enforceable. Women could petition the highest courts. Judges consistently held that married women had autonomy over their own persons. The machinery existed.

Whether the machinery worked for Syra — whether Heraclides summoned Sarapion, whether the dowry was recovered, whether the hemiolion was paid — we do not know.

sections.title: XIV. The vocabulary of a case
sections.body_html:
Consider the precision of Syra’s legal vocabulary.

Phernē — dowry, the specific Greek term for the property a wife brings to a marriage, distinct from proix or parapherna.81 She knew the right word.

Synchōrēsis — cession, the formal registration of a contract through official channels.82 She knew how her dowry had been documented.

Hēmiolia — the fifty-percent surcharge, a standard contractual penalty that appears across centuries of Greco-Egyptian legal practice.83 She knew what she was entitled to under law.

Anegklētos — blameless, without reproach, the same adjective used in formal marriage contracts to describe the expected conduct of both parties: “let both live blamelessly together.”84 She knew the standard she needed to invoke.

Either Syra herself possessed a sophisticated understanding of Greco-Egyptian marriage law, or she had access to a scribe or legal advisor who did. In Oxyrhynchus, professional scribes drafted petitions and contracts for pay. A woman of Syra’s class would likely have known where to find such a person. The petition’s structure follows established conventions: identification of the court, identification of the petitioner, statement of facts, request for remedy, reservation of additional claims.85 It is a document that was written to work.

But beneath the legal framework, something personal persists. The word lithēn — destitute — appears twice: once for Sarapion when he entered the marriage, once for Syra when he left.86 The echo cannot be accidental. It is the verbal equivalent of pointing at the wreckage: he came to me like this, and now look at me.

sections.title: XV. The amicable divorce she could not have
sections.body_html:
What Syra wanted was simple: the return of her money. In 96 CE, half a century or so later, a woman named Thaesis obtained exactly this.

Thaesis, daughter of Thonis, acting with her step-father Onnophris as guardian, “acknowledges to her late husband Petosarapis, son of Thompekusis, son of Sarapion, the receipt from him of the capital sum of 400 silver drachmae which she brought to him with herself as her dowry.”87 The parapherna — her personal property — was also returned. Both parties released all claims. The marriage contract was “returned to him cancelled in order to effect the dissolution of the marriage.”88

This was how it was supposed to work. The husband returns the dowry. The wife returns the contract. Both walk away.

Syra could not have this because Sarapion had already squandered the dowry and then disappeared. There was nothing to return amicably. There was no husband to negotiate with. There was only a petition to a judge in Alexandria and the hope that the state could find one man in a city of perhaps thirty thousand and force him to pay what he no longer had.

A later marriage contract from 260 CE — centuries after Syra, but using identical language — includes the phrase “if — which heaven forbid — in consequence of an estrangement a separation of the parties takes place.”89 Which heaven forbid. The formula acknowledges what everyone knew: marriages ended. Dowries were lost. Couples who had promised to live blamelessly together discovered that blame was easy to come by. The heavens, on this matter, were unreliable.

A remarriage contract from 201 CE shows a couple who had divorced and then remarried, with a child from the first union acknowledged by both: “Let the parties to the marriage therefore live together blamelessly, as they did formerly.”90 As they did formerly. The optimism is almost painful. Perhaps the second time would be different. Or perhaps not.

sections.title: XVI. What we do not know
sections.body_html:
We do not know whether Syra’s petition was ever delivered to Heraclides. We do not know whether Sarapion was summoned. We do not know whether the 300 drachmae was ever paid. We do not know whether Syra returned to her parents’ house or whether her parents were still alive or whether she had children who went hungry when the anankaia stopped.

We do not know whether the “other claims” she reserved — tōn men gar allōn tōn ontōn pros auton antechomai kai anthexomai, “the other things that are between us I hold to and shall hold to” — were ever pursued.91 The Greek is quietly fierce. She is not releasing him. She is keeping her options open. Whatever else he owes her, she will not let go.

We do not know where Sarapion went. The papyri of Roman Egypt are full of people who disappeared — men who “removed abroad,” sons who absconded, husbands who walked away from debts and obligations.92 The bureaucracy tracked these absences when it could, registering the departed for tax purposes. But tracking and finding were different things. A man who left Oxyrhynchus could vanish into the hundreds of villages along the Nile, or into Alexandria’s quarter-million inhabitants, or into the desert.

We do not know Syra’s name in the Egyptian tongue, or whether she had one. Many residents of Oxyrhynchus bore double names — one Greek, one Egyptian — moving between identities depending on which court, which office, which neighbor they were addressing.

We know only what the papyrus tells us. A woman named Syra, daughter of Theon, married a man named Sarapion. She brought money and a house. He brought nothing. She conducted herself blamelessly. He squandered, abused, beat, starved, and left. She wrote it down. Someone filed it. Someone discarded it. Someone, nineteen centuries later, dug it up.

The papyrus was found in a rubbish dump. It may have been a draft, a copy retained for Syra’s records, or a document discarded by the court system after processing.93 The rubbish dumps of Oxyrhynchus were indiscriminate in what they preserved.

But they preserved her voice. Anegklēton emautēn en hāpasi pareichomēn. I conducted myself blamelessly in all respects.

Nobody contradicts her.

notes.text: P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400), dated 20-50 CE. The phrase *anegklēton emautēn en hāpasi pareichomēn* echoes the standard marriage contract formula requiring both parties to live *amemptōs* ("blamelessly"). Cf. P.Oxy. 3.496 (corpus ID 43476), 127 CE, stipulating "let both live blamelessly together"; P.Oxy. 6.905 (corpus ID 46181), 170 CE; P.Oxy. 10.1273 (corpus ID 43201), 260 CE; and P.Oxy. 12.1473 (corpus ID 43247), 201 CE --- all use the same formula across centuries.

P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400). Published in B. P. Grenfell and A. S. Hunt, *The Oxyrhynchus Papyri*, Part II (London: Egypt Exploration Fund, 1899), no. 281. On the Oxyrhynchus excavations, see P. Parsons, *City of the Sharp-Nosed Fish* (London, 2007).
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400). Translation from the editio princeps, lightly adapted.
The editors Grenfell and Hunt dated the document to the early first century CE based on palaeographic grounds. The reign of Tiberius (14-37 CE) or Claudius (41-54 CE) is most likely.

On "ethnic" names in Greco-Roman Egypt that no longer signified actual ethnicity, see W. Clarysse and D. J. Thompson, *Counting the People in Hellenistic Egypt* (Cambridge, 2006), vol. 2, on the fossilization of ethnic categories.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *para Syras tēs Theōnos*.

For Theon as groom, see P.Oxy. 3.497 (corpus ID 43477), early second century CE. For Theon as archidikastes, see P.Oxy. 2.268 (corpus ID 141041), after 57 CE. For Theon son of Demetrius in a land cession, see P.Oxy. 2.347 (corpus ID 43428), ca. 95-100 CE. See also P.Oxy. 2.342 (corpus ID 43424), ca. 100 CE, involving Theon's children.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *eis ta tōn goneōn mou oikētēria*. The use of the plural *goneōn* ("parents") could indicate both parents were alive or simply denote the ancestral family home.

P.Oxy. 2.264 (corpus ID 43389), dated August 8, 54 CE. Tryphon purchases a weaver's loom for 20 silver drachmae, "measuring three weavers' cubits less two palms, and containing two rollers and two beams."
P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401), ca. 50 CE. Sarapion son of Theon reports that Apollophanes "seized from me a linen tunic which I was wearing, worth 8 drachmae."
P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401). Apollophanes extorted "four more drachmae, and two drachmae each month during the six months from the month Neos Sebastos... to Pharmuthi; total, 24 drachmae."

P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391), dated May 22, 37 CE. Tryphon acknowledges receipt from Saraeus of "40 silver drachmae... and for the value of one pair of gold earrings, 20 drachmae of silver, and for a milk-white robe, 12 drachmae of silver, making a total sum of 72 drachmae."
P.Oxy. 2.266 (corpus ID 141040), dated 96 CE. Thaesis receives 400 silver drachmae from her former husband Petosarapis.
P.Oxy. 3.496 (corpus ID 43476), dated April 19, 127 CE. The dowry of 4,100 drachmae included cash, jewelry, and clothing.
P.Oxy. 2.263 is referenced in the research notes for a slave girl valued at 640 drachmae; this comparative figure places Syra's 200-drachma dowry in perspective.

P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *kata synchōrēsin*. The synchōrēsis was a formal agreement registered through an official notarial office. On this type of registration, see H. J. Wolff, *Das Recht der griechischen Papyri Ägyptens* (Munich, 1978).
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400). The husband is identified only as "Sarapion" --- *synebiōsa Sarapiōni*.
P.Oxy. 3.496 (corpus ID 43476), 127 CE. The bride's father is "Sarapion son of Sarapion son of Sarapion son of Sarapion."
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *liton pantelōs onta*. The adjective *litos* means "plain, simple, poor" and with *pantelōs* ("utterly") indicates total destitution.

P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401), ca. 50 CE. The petitioner identifies himself as "Sarapion, son of Theon, a weaver of the city of Oxyrhynchus, living in Gymnasium Square quarter" --- *para Sarapiōnos tou Theōnos tōn ap' Oxyrynchōn poleōs gerdiōn lauras dromou Gymnasiou*.

The patronymic match between "Sarapion son of Theon" (P.Oxy. 2.285, corpus ID 43401) and "Syra daughter of Theon" (P.Oxy. 2.281, corpus ID 43400), combined with their contemporaneous dating and shared city, is suggestive but unconfirmable. Theon was among the most common names in Oxyrhynchus.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400). Greek text from the editio princeps.
The prefix *kata-* in *katachrēsamenos* intensifies the verb *chraomai* ("to use"), producing the sense of "using up," "misusing," or "squandering." Cf. LSJ s.v. *katachraomai*.
P.Oxy. 3.496 (corpus ID 43476), 127 CE: "It shall not be lawful for the husband to sell or mortgage or otherwise dispose of his property without the consent of the bride."

P.Oxy. 2.265 (corpus ID 43390), dated 81-96 CE. The contract includes the clause *mēde kakouchein autēn* --- "nor to ill-treat her" --- using the same root word (*kakoucheō*) that Syra deploys in her petition (*kakouchōn*).

On *hybris* in Greek law, see N. R. E. Fisher, *Hybris: A Study in the Values of Honour and Shame in Ancient Greece* (Warminster, 1992). In Athenian law, *hybris* was prosecutable regardless of the victim's status.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *tas cheiras epipherōn*. The phrase is a standard legal formula for assault; cf. similar language in P.Oxy. 2.283 (corpus ID 40441), 45 CE.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *tōn anankaiōn endeē kathistás*.

P.Oxy. 3.496 (corpus ID 43476), 127 CE: "the husband shall supply the bride with necessaries in proportion to his means." The identical formula appears in P.Oxy. 6.905 (corpus ID 46181), 170 CE: "the husband shall supply the wife with necessaries in proportion to his means." Also P.Oxy. 10.1273 (corpus ID 43201), 260 CE.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *enkatélipe me*.

P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400). Sarapion is described as *liton pantelōs onta* on arrival; Syra describes herself as *lithēn kathestōsan* after his departure. The deliberate echo frames the narrative as a transfer of poverty from husband to wife.

The Tryphon archive (Trismegistos Archive 249) is one of the best-documented artisan archives from Roman Egypt. See J. Whitehorne, "Tryphon's Archive," in *Akten des 21. internationalen Papyrologenkongresses* (Stuttgart, 1997); and E. P. Wegner, "Three Weavers of the First Century CE" (2019).
P.Oxy. 2.282 (corpus ID 141045), dated 29-37 CE. Tryphon complains that Demetrous "became dissatisfied with our union, and finally left the house carrying off property belonging to me."
Both P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400) and P.Oxy. 2.282 (corpus ID 141045) close with the formula: "This petition is without prejudice to the other claims which I have or may have against him/her."
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391), May 22, 37 CE. The dowry was registered "through the bank of Sarapion, son of Kleandrus" at the Serapeum in Oxyrhynchus.
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391): *meth' hēmiolias* --- "with the addition of half its amount."
P.Oxy. 2.251 (corpus ID 43381), dated January 8, 44 CE. Thamounion daughter of Onnophris reports the disappearance of her grandson Thoönis.
P.Oxy. 2.251 (corpus ID 43381). Thoönis is described as *atechnon* ("without trade") and possessing no means --- language similar to Syra's description of Sarapion as *liton pantelōs onta*.
P.Oxy. 1.39 dupl. (corpus ID 28354), dated April 24, 52 CE. Tryphon was examined and released from service by the prefect Gn. Vergilius Capito. The diagnosis: "cataract and shortness of sight."
P.Oxy. 2.264 (corpus ID 43389), August 8, 54 CE. The loom was sold by Ammonius son of Ammonius, transacted "through the bank of Sarapion, son of Lochus, near the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus."

P.Oxy. 2.275 (corpus ID 43394), September 18, 66 CE. Tryphon apprentices his son Thoenis (by Saraeus) to the weaver Ptolemaeus for one year. The contract specifies 5 drachmae per month for food, 12 drachmae for clothing at year's end, and penalties of 1 drachma per missed day and 100 drachmae for early withdrawal.
P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401) and P.Oxy. 2.284 (corpus ID 44673), both ca. 50 CE. Apollophanes is identified as "ex-collector of the trade tax upon weavers."
P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401). Sarapion son of Theon reports Apollophanes's extortion, totaling 24 drachmae plus the seized tunic.
P.Oxy. 2.284 (corpus ID 44673). Alexandrus son of Apollonius reports Apollophanes "extorted from me among other people 16 drachmae of silver."

Tiberius Claudius Pasion served as strategus of the Oxyrhynchite nome during the reign of Claudius (41-54 CE). Both P.Oxy. 2.284 (corpus ID 44673) and P.Oxy. 2.285 (corpus ID 43401) are addressed to him.

P.Oxy. 4.736 (corpus ID 43618), ca. 1 CE. A household account from Oxyrhynchus recording daily expenses in obols: the weaver's breakfast, children's bread and milk, garlands for a birthday celebration.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *Hērakleidēi hierei kai archidikastēi kai pros tēi epimeleiāi tōn chrēmatistōn kai tōn allōn kritēriōn*.

On the archidikastes and his functions, see J. Modrzejewski, "Le droit de la famille dans les lettres privees grecques d'Egypte," *Journal of Juristic Papyrology* 9-10 (1955-56). The archidikastes supervised contract registration, court proceedings, and the public records office (katalogeion).

P.Oxy. 12.1471 (corpus ID 43245), December 31, 81 CE. The archidikastes is "Pallas, priest, archidicastes, and superintendent of the chrematistae and the other courts." The document involves a loan of 38 drachmae with hemiolion penalty.
P.Oxy. 2.268 (corpus ID 141041), after November 29, 57 CE. Addressed to "Theon, chief justice and superintendent of the chrematistae and the other courts." A dowry of 800 drachmae is at issue.
P.Oxy. 12.1475 (corpus ID 43248), March 20, 267 CE. The archidikastes is "Aurelius Gaius also called Harpocration, priest, archidicastes, and superintendent of the chrematistae and other courts."
On the chrematistae courts, see H. J. Wolff, *Das Justizwesen der Ptolemäer* (Munich, 1962). The three court systems were established under Ptolemy II Philadelphus (282-246 BCE).

By the Roman period, the chrematistae had absorbed the jurisdiction of both the dikasteria (Greek courts) and the laokritai (Egyptian courts). The archidikastes served as chief administrator of this consolidated system.

Syra's choice to petition the archidikastes rather than the strategus may reflect the nature of her dowry registration (*kata synchōrēsin*), which could have been processed through the archidikastes's office. The strategus Alexandrus received Tryphon's petition (P.Oxy. 2.282, corpus ID 141045) and Tiberius Claudius Pasion received the weavers' petitions --- suggesting that the strategus handled most local complaints.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *apodounai moi tēn phernēn syn hēmioliāi*.
P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391), 37 CE: *meth' hēmiolias*.
P.Oxy. 3.496 (corpus ID 43476), 127 CE. The hemiolion clause applies to the 4,100-drachma dowry.
P.Oxy. 3.497 (corpus ID 43477), early second century CE: *ē apoteisatō meth' hēmiolias*.
P.Oxy. 12.1471 (corpus ID 43245), 81 CE. A loan of 38 drachmae with hemiolion penalty and provisions for "arrest and detention."

P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *axiō syntaxai katastēsai auton epi se hopōs epanankasthēi synechomenos apodounai*. The phrase *synechomenos* --- "being held" or "under compulsion" --- implies physical detention.
On the guardianship requirement in Greco-Roman Egypt, see J. Rowlandson, *Women and Society in Greek and Roman Egypt* (Cambridge, 1998), ch. 4.
P.Oxy. 2.266 (corpus ID 141040), 96 CE. Thaesis acts "with her guardian her step-father Onnophris."
P.Oxy. 2.268 (corpus ID 141041), after 57 CE.
P.Oxy. 2.273 (corpus ID 43392), June 24, 95 CE. Julia Heracla acts with a guardian assigned by the late prefect Gaius Septimius Vegetus.
P.Oxy. 74.4983 (corpus ID 39488), ca. 76 CE. Sieapis daughter of Sarapion acts "with her son Ariston son of Diogenes as guardian."
On the *ius liberorum* in Roman Egypt, see J. Rowlandson, *Women and Society in Greek and Roman Egypt* (Cambridge, 1998), ch. 4.
P.Oxy. 12.1475 (corpus ID 43248), 267 CE, shows a woman "acting without a guardian by the ius liberorum."
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400). The first-person verbs throughout the petition present Syra as the agent of her own narrative.

P.Oxy. 2.282 (corpus ID 141045): "I for my part provided for my wife in a manner that exceeded my resources." Compare P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): "I for my part conducted myself blamelessly in all respects."
P.Oxy. 2.282 (corpus ID 141045), dated 29-37 CE on the basis of the strategus Alexandrus, who is named as addressee.
P.Oxy. 2.282 (corpus ID 141045). Tryphon's list of stolen property begins with an item worth 40 drachmae.
ZPE 176.213 (corpus ID 11799), ca. 156-125 BCE. Senyris petitions about her partner: "He threw me out, and he doesn't even feed or clothe us."
P.Oxy. 2.286 (corpus ID 43402), May 17, 82 CE. The petitioner reports that "Philumene is continually pressing me to repay" the 2,000 drachmae owed to her.

P.Oxy. 10.1272 (corpus ID 43200), November 10, 144 CE. Diemous daughter of Colluthus reports the theft of gold bracelets, a gold figure of Bes, and silver bracelets, suspecting her neighbor Heras the weaver.
P.Oxy. 2.283 (corpus ID 40441), August 8, 45 CE. A petition describing violence during the seizure of a slave named Euporus, addressed to Tiberius Claudius Pasion.

P.Oxy. 2.294 (corpus ID 28356), December 11, 22 CE. A personal letter from Sarapion to his brother Dorion, giving a vivid picture of daily concerns: property, legal proceedings, social networks, and domestic care.
P.Oxy. 2.237 (corpus ID 141383), second century CE. The dossier of Dionysia vs. her father Chaeremon is one of the longest and most complex judicial documents from Roman Egypt.
P.Oxy. 2.237 (corpus ID 141383). Extract from the minutes of Flavius Titianus, sometime prefect.
P.Oxy. 2.237 (corpus ID 141383). Lawyer's opinion cited in the dossier.

P.Oxy. 2.237 (corpus ID 141383). Precedent cited: "Probatianus on behalf of Antonius added that if the marriage was not cancelled the father had no power over the dowry any more than over the daughter whom he had given in marriage."

On the distinction between *phernē* (dowry), *proix* (gift), and *parapherna* (personal property beyond the dowry), see A. Arjava, *Women and Law in Late Antiquity* (Oxford, 1996), pp. 52-62. P.Oxy. 2.266 (corpus ID 141040) distinguishes between the phernē and the parapherna in an amicable divorce.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *kata synchōrēsin*. On the synchōrēsis, see Wolff, *Das Recht*, vol. 2.

The hemiolion appears in P.Oxy. 2.267 (corpus ID 43391), P.Oxy. 3.496 (corpus ID 43476), P.Oxy. 3.497 (corpus ID 43477), and P.Oxy. 12.1471 (corpus ID 43245), spanning the 1st through 3rd centuries CE.

P.Oxy. 3.496 (corpus ID 43476): "let both live blamelessly together." The same requirement appears in P.Oxy. 6.905 (corpus ID 46181), P.Oxy. 10.1273 (corpus ID 43201), and P.Oxy. 12.1473 (corpus ID 43247).

The petition follows the standard format: (1) addressee with full title, (2) petitioner's identification by name and patronymic, (3) statement of facts in narrative order, (4) request for specific remedy, (5) reservation of additional claims. Cf. P.Oxy. 2.286 (corpus ID 43402), which uses the identical closing formula.
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400). The word *litos/lithē* appears twice: *liton pantelōs onta* (Sarapion) and *lithēn kathestōsan* (Syra after his departure).
P.Oxy. 2.266 (corpus ID 141040), 96 CE. Thaesis receives 400 drachmae from Petosarapis, "son of Thompekusis, son of Sarapion."
P.Oxy. 2.266 (corpus ID 141040): "This bond she has thereupon returned to him cancelled in order to effect the dissolution of the marriage."
P.Oxy. 10.1273 (corpus ID 43201), January 28, 260 CE: "if --- which heaven forbid --- in consequence of an estrangement a separation of the parties takes place."
P.Oxy. 12.1473 (corpus ID 43247), June 4, 201 CE: "Let the parties to the marriage therefore live together blamelessly, as they did formerly."
P.Oxy. 2.281 (corpus ID 43400): *tōn men gar allōn tōn ontōn pros auton antechomai kai anthexomai*. The future tense *anthexomai* signals ongoing intention --- she will continue to hold her claims.

See P.Oxy. 2.251 (corpus ID 43381), 44 CE, where Thamounion reports her grandson as having "removed abroad." The registration was necessary for tax purposes: if Thoönis had absconded, the family would otherwise be liable for his taxes.
The document was excavated from the rubbish dumps of Oxyrhynchus by Grenfell and Hunt. Whether it is a draft, a retained copy, or a discarded court filing cannot be determined.

sources.description: Petition | Syra vs. Sarapion: dowry squandered, abuse, desertion
sources.greek: Ἡρακλείδηι ἱερεῖ καὶ ἀρχιδι Ἡρακλείδηι ἱερεῖ καὶ ἀρχιδι καστῆι καὶ πρὸς τῇ ἐπιμε καστῆι καὶ πρὸς τῇ ἐπιμε λείᾳ τῶν χρηματιστῶν καὶ τῶ λείᾳ τῶν χρηματιστῶν καὶ τῶ ν ἄλλων κριτηρίων ἄλλων κριτηρίων παρὰ Σύρας τῆς Θέωνος. παρὰ Σύρας τῆς Θέωνος. συνεβίωσα Σαραπίωνι φερνὴν το συνεβίωσα Σαραπίωνι φερνὴν το ύ τῳ δοῦσα κατὰ συνχώρησιν εἰς τῳ δοῦσα κατὰ συνχώρησιν εἰς λόγον ἀργυρίου δραχμῶν διακο λόγον ἀργυρίου δραχμῶν διακο σί ων ων . ἐγὼ μὲν οὖν ἐπιδεξαμέ νη αὐτὸν εἰς τὰ τῶν γονέων νη αὐτὸν εἰς τὰ τῶν γονέων μου οἰκητήρια μου οἰκητήρια λιτὸν λειτὸν παν τελῶς ὄντα ἀνέγκλητον τελῶς ὄντα ἀνέγκλητον ἐμαυτὴν ἐματὴν ἐν ἁπᾶσι ἁπᾶσει παρειχό μην. ὁ δὲ Σαραπίων κατα μην. ὁ δὲ Σαραπίων κατα χρη χρη σά μενος τῆι φερνῇ εἰς ὃν ἐβούλετο ἠβούλετο λόγον οὐ διέλει πεν κακουχῶν με καὶ ὑβρί πεν κακουχῶν με καὶ ὑβρί ζ ων καὶ τὰς χεῖρας ἐπι φέρων καὶ τῶν ἀναγκαί φέρων καὶ τῶν ἀναγκαί ων ἐνδεῆ καθιστάς, ὕσ ων ἐνδεῆ καθιστάς, ὕσ τερον δὲ ἐνκατέ τερον δὲ ἐνκατέ λιπέ με λιπέ με λιτὴν λειτὴν καθεσ τῶσαν. διὸ ἀξιῶ συντάξαι τῶσαν. διὸ ἀξιῶ συντάξαι καταστῆσαι αὐτὸν ἐπὶ σὲ καταστῆσαι αὐτὸν ἐπὶ σὲ ὅπως ἐπαναγκασθῇ συν ὅπως ἐπαναγκασθῇ συν εχόμενος ἀποδοῦναι εχόμενος ἀποδοῦναι μ μοι τὴν μοι τὴν φ ερνὴν σὺν ἡμι ολίᾳ. τῶ ολίᾳ. τῶ ν μὲν γὰρ ἄλ λων τῶν λων τῶν ὄντων πρὸς αὐτὸν ἀντέχομ ἀντέχομ αι καὶ ἀνθέξομαι .
sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Heraclides, priest, chief justice, superintendent of the chrematistae and the other courts, from Syra, daughter of Theon. I married Sarapion, bringing him by cession a dowry amounting to 200 drachmae of silver. As he was destitute of means I received him into my parents’ house, and I for my part conducted myself blamelessly in all respects. But Sarapion, having squandered my dowry as he pleased, continually ill-treated and insulted me, using violence towards me, and depriving me of the necessaries of life; finally he deserted me leaving me in a state of destitution. I therefore beg you to order him to be brought before you, in order that he may be compelled perforce to pay back my dowry increased by half its amount. This petition is without prejudice to any other claims which I have or may have against him.

sources.description: Petition | Sarapion son of Theon, weaver, extorted by Apollophanes
sources.greek: Τιβερίωι Κλαυδίῳ Πασίων Τιβερίωι Κλαυδίῳ Πασίων ι στρ ατηγῷ παρὰ Σαραπίωνος τοῦ Θέωνος παρὰ Σαραπίωνος τοῦ Θέωνος τῶν ἀπʼ Ὀξυρύγχων πόλεως τῶν ἀπʼ Ὀξυρύγχων πόλεως γερδίων λαύρας δρόμου Γυμν γερδίων λαύρας δρόμου Γυμν α σίου. Ἀπολλοφάνης γενόμ σίου. Ἀπολλοφάνης γενόμ ενος πράκτωρ πράκτωρ χειρωναξίου χιρωναξίου γερ δίων τῷ δίων τῷ α ἔτει Τιβερίου Κλαυδ ίου Καίσαρος Σεβαστοῦ Γερμανικοῦ Καίσαρος Σεβαστοῦ Γερμανικοῦ Αὐτοκρά Αὐτοκρά τ ορος πολλῇ βίᾳ χρώ μενος ἀφήρπασεν ὃν ἤμην μενος ἀφήρπασεν ὃν ἤμην ἐνδεδυμένο ς ἐνδεδυμενο χιτῶνα λινοῦν λει νοῦν ἄξι ο ν ἀξιν δραχμῶν ὀκτώ , καὶ διέ σεισέν σεισέν διέ σισέν σισέν με ἄλλας δραχμὰς τέσσα ρας ρας τέσσα ρες ρες , καὶ ἀπὸ μηνὸς Νέου Σεβασ τοῦ ἐνάτου ἔτους Τιβερίου τοῦ ἐνάτου ἔτους Τιβερίου Κλαυδίου Κλαυδδίου Καίσαρος Σεβαστοῦ Γερμανικοῦ Αὐτοκράτορος ἕως Γερμανικοῦ Αὐτοκράτορος ἕως Φαρμοῦθι, μηνῶν ἕξ Φαρμοῦθι, μηνῶν ἕξ , κατὰ μῆνα δραχμὰς δύο δραχμὰς δύο , αἳ συναγόμεναι δραχμαὶ κδ . διὸ ἀξιῶ διαλαβεῖν κατʼ αὐτοῦ διὸ ἀξιῶ διαλαβεῖν κατʼ αὐτοῦ ὡς ἐάν σοι φαίνηται. εὐτύχει. ὡς ἐάν σοι φαίνηται. εὐτύχει. θεουκαιπιαπενεκαιαγυνι καπιουγενεπιν καπιουγενεπιν κ ινκαπι σεουκαισοφωνεκαισο σεουκαισοφωνεκαισο ... ο υκαισεραθευκαις ουκαιουνεσουκαισθευπιθος ουκαιουνεσουκαισθευπιθος καισουκαισουσπουκαισθ καισουκαισουσουκαισθ
sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Tiberius Claudius Pasion, strategus, from Sarapion, son of Theon, a weaver of the city of Oxyrhynchus, living in Gymnasium square quarter. Apollophanes, ex-collector of the trade tax upon weavers, in the first year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator using great violence seized from me a linen tunic which I was wearing, worth 8 drachmae. He also extorted from me four more drachmae, and two drachmae each month during the six months from the month Neos Sebastos in the ninth year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator to Pharmuthi; total, 24 drachmae. I therefore beg you to proceed against him as you may think fit.

sources.description: Petition | Alexandrus the weaver, extorted by Apollophanes
sources.greek: Τιβερίωι Κλαυδίωι Τιβερίωι Κλαυδίωι Πασί ωνι στρα τηγῷ παρὰ Ἀλεξάνδρου τοῦ παρὰ Ἀλεξάνδρου τοῦ Ἀπολ λωνίου τ ῶν ἀπʼ Ὀξυρύγχων πόλεω ς γερ δίων λαύρας δρόμου Θοήριδος. sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Tiberius Claudius Pasion, strategus, from Alexandrus, son of Apollonius, a weaver of Oxyrhynchus, living in the quarter of the square of Thoëris. Apollophanes, ex-collector of taxes, in the eighth year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator extorted from me among other people 16 drachmae of silver. I therefore beg you to proceed against him as you may think fit.

sources.description: Petition | Tryphon vs. first wife Demetrous: stolen property

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Alexandrus, strategus, from Tryphon, son of Dionysius, of the city of Oxyrhynchus. I married Demetrous, daughter of Heraclides, and I for my part provided for my wife in a manner that exceeded my resources. But she became dissatisfied with our union, and finally left the house carrying off property belonging to me a list of which is added below. I beg, therefore, that she be brought before you in order that she may receive her deserts, and return to me my property. This petition is without prejudice to the other claims which I have or may have against her. The stolen articles are :— a... worth 40 drachmae...

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              Tryphon, son of Dionysius, a Persian of the Epigone, to Saraeus, daughter of Apion, under the wardship of Onnophris, son of Antipater, greeting. I acknowledge the receipt from you at the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus through the bank of Sarapion, son of Kleandrus, of 40 silver drachmae of the Imperial and Ptolemaic coinage, and for the value of one pair of gold earrings, 20 drachmae of silver, and for a milk-white robe, 12 drachmae of silver, making a total sum of 72 drachmae of silver, to which nothing at all has been added, in consideration of which I have consented (to our marriage). And I will repay to you the
              72 drachmae of silver on the 30th of Phaophi in the coming second year of Gaius Caesar Germanicus Novus Augustus Imperator without any delay. If I do not repay in accordance with the above terms I will forfeit to you the said sum with the addition of half its amount, for which you are to have the right of execution upon me and upon all my property, as in accordance with a legal decision. If we separate from each other, you shall be empowered to have the pair of earrings at their present value. And since we are living together without a marriage contract, I further agree if as aforesaid owing to a quarrel we separate from each other while you are in a state of pregnancy, to ... so long as you ... This receipt is valid wherever and by whomsoever it is produced.
              There follow (1) the signature of Tryphon, written for him by Leon, (2) the signature of Onnophris, the guardian of Saraeus, written on his behalf by Theon, son of Paaeis, (3) the docket of the bank through which the payment was made, (4) the signature of Saraeus, written for her, in astonishingly badly spelled Greek, by Didymus, son of Boëthus, acknowledging that she had received back the sum mentioned in the agreement. This acknowledgement of Saraeus is dated Payni 15 in the 3rd year of Claudius.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              Ammonius, son of Ammonius, to Tryphon, son of Dionysius, greeting. I agree that I have sold to you the weaver’s loom belonging to me, measuring three weavers’ cubits less two palms, and containing two rollers and two beams, and I acknowledge the receipt from you through the bank of Sarapion, son of Lochus, near the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus, of the price of it agreed upon between us, namely 20 silver drachmae of the Imperial and Ptolemaic coinage; and that I will guarantee to you the sale with every guarantee, under penalty of payment to you of the price which I have received from you increased by half its amount, and of the damages. This note of hand is valid. The 14th year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, the 15th of the month Caesareus.
              I, Ammonius, son of Ammonius, have sold the loom, and have received the price of 20 drachmae of silver and will guarantee the sale as aforesaid. I, Heraclides, son of Dionysius, wrote for him as he was illiterate.
              Date, and banker’s signature.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              Agreement between Tryphon, son of Dionysius, son of Tryphon, his mother being Thamounis, daughter of Onnophris, and Ptolemaeus, weaver, son of Pausirion, son of Ptolemaeus, his mother being Ophelous, daughter of Theon, both parties being inhabitants of the city of Oxyrhynchus. Tryphon agrees that he has apprenticed to Ptolemaeus his son Thoënis, whose mother is Saraeus, daughter of Apion, and who is not yet of age, for a term of one year from this day, to serve and to perform all the orders given him by Ptolemaeus in respect of his weaver’s art in all its branches of which Ptolemaeus has knowledge. The boy is to be fed and clothed during the whole period by his father Tryphon, who is also to be responsible for all the taxes upon him, on condition of a monthly payment to himself by Ptolemaeus of 5 drachmae on account of victuals, and at the termination of the whole period of a payment of 12 drachmae on account of clothing. Tryphon is not to have the power of taking away his son from Ptolemaeus until the completion of the period; and if there are any days on which the boy fails to attend, Tryphon shall produce him for an equivalent number of days after the period is over, or shall forfeit for each day 1 drachma of silver. The penalty for taking him away within the period shall be 100 drachmae, and an equal sum to the treasury. If Ptolemaeus fails to instruct the boy thoroughly he is to be liable to the same penalties. This contract of apprenticeship is valid.
              Date, and signature of Ptolemaeus.

sources.description: Release | Tryphon released from service: cataract, poor sight

sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 Copy of a release dated and signed in the twelfth year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator, Pharmouthi 29. Release from service was granted by Gn. Vergilius Capito, praefect of Upper and Lower Egypt, to Tryphon, son of Dionysius, weaver, suffering from cataract and shortness of sight, of the metropolis of Oxyrhynchus. Examination was made in Alexandria.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              To Didymus and..., topogrammateis and komogrammateis, from Thamounion, daughter of Onnophris, of the city of Oxyrhynchus, with her guardian Sarapion, son of Sarapion. My son Thoönis, son of Dionysius, who has no trade, registered in the quarter of Temouenouthis, some time ago removed abroad. Wherefore I ask that his name be entered in the list of persons removed, henceforth from this year which is the 4th of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Imperator; and I swear by Tiberius Claudius, etc., that the aforesaid statement is correct, and that Thoönis possesses no means... If I swear truly may it be well with me, but if falsely the reverse. Farewell.
              Signature of Thamounion, written by her guardian, date, and official description of Thamounion’s age and appearance.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              The 16th year of the Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, on the ... of the month Germanicus, at the city of Oxyrhynchus in the Thebaid. Thaësis, daughter of Thonis, son of Amithonis, her mother being Sintheus, with her guardian her step-father Onnophris, son of Onnophris, son of Pammenes, his mother being Taarthonis, acknowledges to her late husband Petosarapis, son of Thompekusis, son of Sarapion, his mother being Sinthonis, all of Oxyrhynchus (the agreement being executed in the street), the receipt from him of the capital sum of 400 silver drachmae of the Imperial coinage which she brought to him with herself as her dowry and for which his mother Sinthonis, daughter of Petosarapis, son of ..., gave a joint guarantee, in accordance with a contract of marriage drawn up through the office of the agoranomi at Oxyrhynchus on the intercalary days of the 14th year of the Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus. This bond she has thereupon returned to him cancelled in order to effect the dissolution of the marriage; and she neither makes nor will make any claim, nor will proceed against him either on account of the aforesaid sum or of the parapherna (which she has also received) or of anything else up to the present date. Petosarapis likewise on his part acknowledges, in the same street, that he neither makes nor will make any claim, nor will proceed against Thaësis or any of her agents on any account whatsoever up to the present date...

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 2
              Copy. To Theon, chief justice and superintendent of the chrematistae and the other courts, from Ammonarion, daughter of Ammonius, son of Dionysius, and however else she is described at Ptolemais Hermiu, and from her daughter Ophelous, whose father is Heraclas, of Oxyrhynchus, the two women acting with their guardian, the half brother of Ammonarion on the mother’s side, Besarion, son of Heras, and however else he is described at Ptolemais, and from ... Antiphanes, son of Ammonius, of the said city of Oxyrhynchus. We agree with each other as follows :— Ammonarion and Ophelous have given their consent and have received from Antiphanes from hand to hand in cash the sum which they severally consented to accept, Ammonarion, on account of the dowry, amounting to 800 silver drachmae, which she brought to her late husband, the brother of Antiphanes’ father and the father of Ophelous, Heraclas, son of Antiphanes, of the same city of Oxyrhynchus, in accordance with a settlement completed some time ago through the daybook, and Ophelous on her part resigns to Antiphanes her share of all the property left by her late father Heraclas. The said agreement of marriage is void, and neither Ammonarion nor Ophelous nor any one acting on their behalf has any further claim against Antiphanes or against the property left by Heraclas, Ammonarion on account of the refunded dowry, and Ophelous on account of the resigned inheritance, as is aforesaid; and neither of them has any claim respecting any other matter whatever written or unwritten of past date down to the present day, and any claim that is made shall be void and inadmissible. The above agreement has no ..., for which we make due petition. Date.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 3
              The 11th year of the Emperor Caesar Trajanus Hadrianus Augustus, Pharmouthi 24, at Oxyrhynchus in the Thebaid, for good fortune, on the day of Julia Augusta, in the street. Sarapion son of Sarapion son of Sarapion son of Sarapion, his mother being Thais daughter of Sarapion, of Oxyrhynchus, has given in marriage his daughter Thais whose mother is ... to Sarapion son of Eudaemon son of Theon, his mother being Heras daughter of ... and Didous, who has received from Sarapion, the father and giver of the bride, a pair of ... weighing 3 minae 14 1/2 quarters, a brooch of 8 quarters, a ... of 6 quarters, a chain with 3 green ... of stone, the gold weighing [.] 1/2 quarters, making altogether on the standard of Oxyrhynchus 5 minae ... quarters, also 2 dresses, 2 girdles, one red the other rose-colored, a ... and a mantle, together worth 560 silver drachmae, and 1860 silver drachmae, the total value of the whole dowry being 4100 drachmae of silver of the Imperial coinage. Besides this the grandmother of the bride, Thais daughter of Sarapion and Heraclous, of the same city, with her guardian who is another son of hers and the full brother of the giver of the bride, Sarapion son of Sarapion, acknowledges in the same street that she has given away Thais in marriage, and she confers upon the said Thais (the possession of the slave) Callityche and her future offspring, the services of and the profits from her to be shared by the husband with the bride so long as they live together; and it shall not be lawful for the husband to ... the slave without his wife’s consent nor anything that is brought to him by his wife, nor to sell or mortgage or otherwise dispose of his property namely a house, yard and court and its fixtures and his slaves Sarapous and Nicarous and the children of Nicarous, Sarapous and Cerdon and Epicharmus, and the future offspring of them or ethers, or any additional property which he may acquire, without the consent of the bride. Let both live blamelessly together, and the husband shall supply the bride with necessaries in proportion to his means; but if any difference arises between them and the bride wishes to separate from her husband, as soon as the separation takes place the bride shall withdraw the slave Callityche and the children that may be born to her, and the husband shall repay to the giver of the bride if he survives, and if not, to the bride herself, the 4100 drachmae of the dowry within ... days from the day on which they are demanded or forfeit this amount increased by one half... And if the bride is at the time of separation in a state of pregnancy the husband shall give her on account of the birth 60 drachmae more. When they come together may they enjoy health; but if either husband or wife should chance to die, the husband shall have power over his own property to make any further provisions he pleases and to divide it among whom he will; but if he makes no further provisions the property shall after his death belong to their children. If the husband dies first the bride shall have... and she or her nearest relation on the one part and whoever shall be appointed by the husband on the other part shall together be guardians, the children being brought up with their mother until they come of age. If the husband appoints no guardian for the one part of the guardianship the bride or her nearest of kin shall act alone, and no one shall be permitted to deprive her of the guardianship nor any part of it. If the bride dies first without their having any children or when those that have been born have died childless, the husband shall repay the dowry namely ... the 4100 drachmae of silver in 60 days and shall send to the said relations of the bride all the rest of her property. Similarly if the husband dies first without their having any children or when those that have been born have died childless, the bride shall ... and withdraw the slave Callityche and the children that may be born to her, and until she has recovered them she shall have control over the whole property, and with regard to all the provisions the choice shall rest with the bride to have either if she prefers the aforesaid gold ornaments included in the dowry at the same weight or their equivalent value, and the bride and her agents shall have the right of execution upon both the husband and upon all his property in accordance with their agreement with each other. The certifier of both parties is Diogenes son of Hierax, scribe, of the same city, in the same street.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 6
              ... Antoninus and Faustina, Augusti. Menodorus son of Horus and Tacallippus of the village of Psobthis has given for partnership of marriage Thatres, his daughter by Thatres, to Apollonius son of Heracles and Tausorapis. The bride brings to her husband in respect of dowry one mina’s weight on the Oxyrhynchite standard of common gold, in kind, according to valuation, and in parapherna in clothing two outer veils, one ..., the other white. Let the husband and wife therefore live together observing the duties of marriage, and the husband shall supply the wife with necessaries in proportion to his means. If a separation takes place after the birth of children or before it, the husband shall restore all the super-dowry at the time of separation, and the dowry in sixty days from the day on which the separation takes place; and Menodorus, the giver of the bride, shall have the right of execution upon the husband and upon all his property. The father of the husband, Heracles son of Morus and Apollonia, of the said village, being present assents to the marriage, and is surety for the payment of the aforesaid dowry. This contract is valid, being written in duplicate in order that each party may have a copy; and in answer to the formal question they have declared to each other their consent. The 10th year, Phamenoth 18.

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 10
              For good fortune. Aurelia Thaësis daughter of Eudaemon and Heraïs, of Oxyrhynchus, acting with Aurelius Theon also called Nepotianus and however he is styled, has given her daughter Aurelia Tausiris in marriage to the husband Aurelius Arsinoiis son of Tryphon and Demetria, of the said city, to whom the said giver contributes as the dowry of her said daughter the bride in common gold on the Oxyrhynchite standard a necklace of the kind called maniaces, having a stone and weighing apart from the stone 13 quarters, a brooch (?) with 5 stones set in gold, weighing apart from the stones 4 quarters, a pair of ear-rings with 10 pearls weighing apart from the pearls 3 quarters, a small ring weighing 4 quarter, and in clothing at a valuation a silvery striped Dalmatian veil worth 260 drachmae, a white, single, tasselled, striped frock worth 160 drachmae, a turquoise-coloured Dalmatian veil worth 100 drachmae, another white Dalmatian veil with a purple border worth 100 drachmae, making the total of the whole dowry 1 mina 4 1/2 quarters of common gold, and for the valuation of the clothing 620 drachmae, a sum total to which no addition has been made; and questioned concerning the aforesaid dowry by the giver of the bride Aurelia Thaësis, the bridegroom Aurelius Arsinoüs agreed that he had received the full number at the aforesaid weight and valuation. Let husband and wife therefore live blamelessly together, observing the duties of marriage, and the husband shall supply his wife with al necessaries in proportion to his means; but if—which heaven forbid—in consequence of an estrangement a separation of the parties takes place, the husband shall restore to the giver of the bride, if she be living, or if not, to the bride, the aforesaid dowry in full within 60 days from the day on which a demand for restoration is made, the gold objects in accordance with the amount of the weight in each case. while, in respect of the clothes at a valuation, the bride’s representatives shall have the choice of keeping them at the valuation to be then made and receiving the balance in silver, or receiving the aforesaid valuation, the responsibility for the wear and loss of all these resting with the husband. If at the time of the separation the bride should be pregnant, the husband shall give her on account of the expenses of the birth 40 drachmae; and in connexion with the demand of the aforesaid dowry the bride’s representatives shall have the right of execution upon both the husband and all his property. This contract is valid,being written in duplicate so that each party may have one copy, and whenever they or one of them chooses, he shall make it public through the bureau, without requiring the concurrence of the other side or any further consent, because both sides now agree to the future publication, and to each other’s questions whether this is done rightly and fairly they have given their assent. The 7th year of the Emperors and Caesars Publius Licinius Valerianus and Publius Licinius Valerianus Gallienus Germanici Maximi Pii Felices and Publius Licinius Cornclius Saloninus Valerianus, the most noble Caesar, Augusti, Mecheir 2. (Signed) I, Aurelia Thaësis, have given my daughter in marriage to the above mentioned Arsinoüs and have made over to him the aforesaid dowry asaforesaid,and in answer to the formal question have declared my consent. I, Aurelius Theon also called Nepotianus, was associated with her and wrote on her behalf, as she is illiterate. I, Aurelius Arsinoüs, have received the aforesaid dowry, and if—which heaven forbid—a separation take place, I will restore it as aforesaid, and in answer to the formal question I have declared my consent.

sources.description: Contract | Remarriage with dowry: "live together blamelessly"
